title: Parse two unsigned integers and write their sum, or error on parse failure or u32 overflow
prompt: Read two lines from standard input. Each line contains an unsigned decimal integer that fits in u32 (range 0 to 4294967295 inclusive). Parse both. If either parse fails (empty line, non-digit characters, leading sign, leading zero is OK, value exceeds u32 max), write the literal string `error\n` to standard output and exit. If both parse but `a + b` would exceed 4294967295 (u32 overflow), write `error\n` to standard output and exit. Otherwise write the sum as decimal followed by a single newline. Do not write to standard error. Exit with status 0 in every case. Trailing whitespace on the digit lines is tolerated (trimmed); a stray leading `+` is not accepted as valid.

input_schema.stdin: two lines: addend a (u32), addend b (u32)

output_schema.stdout: either `<decimal_sum>\n` on success or `error\n` on any failure

notes_input_convention: Zero reads a from argv[1] and b from argv[2] because Zero 0.1.2 has no exposed stdin capability; the values are interpreted identically to the two stdin lines other languages read (trailing whitespace trimmed). All other languages read two newline-terminated lines from stdin. This task closes the error-handling arc: three independent failure modes (parse-a, parse-b, u32-add-overflow) all collapse to the same `error\n` output. Rust uses `u32::checked_add`; Go compares `uint64(a)+uint64(b)` against `math.MaxUint32`; TypeScript/Python compare `a + b > 4294967295` directly; Zero accumulates the sum in u64 and bounds-checks against `4294967295_u64`.
